q: What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and why would you use it instead of just relying on the LLM's built-in knowledge?
a: RAG connects an LLM to an external knowledge source at query time: it retrieves relevant documents, injects them into the prompt as context, and the model generates a grounded answer from that context. It solves three problems pure LLMs have — knowledge cutoff, hallucination, and lack of access to private data. Unlike fine-tuning, which encodes knowledge into weights and is expensive to update, RAG is the right choice when knowledge changes frequently, must be auditable with sources, or lives in private documents. The pipeline has four stages: ingest, index, retrieve, and generate.
fj: RAG is just better than fine-tuning, always use it.
fs: RAG is better when knowledge is dynamic or needs to be cited; fine-tuning is better for stable behavioral changes. They solve different problems and teams often combine both.

followups.q: When would you choose fine-tuning over RAG?
followups.a: When the knowledge is stable and doesn't need to be cited — for example, teaching the model to respond in a consistent brand voice, follow a custom output format, or reason in a specialized domain (medical coding, legal clause classification). Fine-tuning encodes behavior into weights; RAG injects knowledge at inference time. They solve different problems.

followups.q: What happens if no relevant documents are retrieved?
followups.a: The LLM generates with no grounding context, which is basically back to relying on parametric memory — hallucination risk spikes. Good systems either detect this (low similarity score on all retrieved chunks) and respond 'I don't have enough information,' or guard against it with a fallback prompt instruction like 'if the context doesn't contain the answer, say so explicitly.'

followups.q: What is a vector embedding and why does retrieval use it?
followups.a: An embedding is a dense numeric vector (e.g., 1536 floats) that represents a piece of text as a point in high-dimensional space, such that semantically similar texts are geometrically close. Retrieval computes the embedding of the query and finds document chunks whose embeddings are nearest — this captures semantic similarity, not just keyword overlap. A search for 'car repair cost' will find 'automobile maintenance pricing' even without shared words.

q: Walk me through how chunking works in a RAG pipeline and how you would pick a chunk size.
a: Chunking splits source documents into smaller segments before embedding, because embedding models have token limits and smaller, focused chunks produce more precise embeddings. Too-small chunks lose context and become fragments; too-large chunks blend multiple topics and dilute retrieval precision while consuming more of the context window. A typical starting point is 300-500 tokens with 10-20% overlap, where overlap prevents answers from being split across chunk boundaries. Chunk size is a hyperparameter you should validate by measuring retrieval recall@k on a labeled test set, not a constant.
fj: I'd use whatever the default chunk size is.
fs: I'd start at 300-500 tokens with overlap, then validate the choice by measuring retrieval recall@k on a labeled test set — chunk size is a hyperparameter, not a constant.

followups.q: How do you choose between fixed-size and semantic chunking?
followups.a: Start with fixed-size (it's simple and fast to implement), measure retrieval recall@k on a labeled test set, and switch to semantic chunking if recall is insufficient. Semantic chunking adds ingestion compute cost and complexity — it only pays for itself if you can measure a quality improvement.

followups.q: How do you handle documents with natural structure — like PDFs with headers and sections?
followups.a: Respect the structure: split at section boundaries (headings, paragraphs) rather than fixed token counts. Store the section title and document metadata as chunk metadata so the retriever can also filter by section or document type. Forcing fixed-size chunks across section breaks destroys the structural signal.

q: What is top-k retrieval in a RAG system? How do you pick k, and what goes wrong at the extremes?
a: Top-k retrieval fetches the k document chunks with the highest vector similarity to the query embedding, and those chunks become the context injected into the prompt. Too small a k is brittle and hurts recall — if the closest chunk misses a nuance, the model has nothing to fall back on. Too large a k fills the prompt with marginally relevant chunks that distract the model, degrade answers, and raise context-window cost. A typical start is k=3-5; tune empirically by measuring precision@k and answer quality, and consider reranking rather than blindly increasing k.
fj: I'd set k as high as the context window allows.
fs: More chunks doesn't mean better answers — irrelevant chunks add noise. I'd start at k=3-5, measure retrieval precision and answer quality, and rerank rather than blindly increasing k.

followups.q: How do you measure whether your k is right?
followups.a: Two metrics: retrieval recall@k (is the ground-truth document in the top k?) on a labeled test set, and end-to-end answer quality (LLM-graded or human). If recall@k is low, k is too small or the retriever is weak. If recall is high but answer quality is low, the problem may be generation or irrelevant chunks polluting the prompt.

q: RAG vs fine-tuning vs prompting — how do you decide which approach to use for a new LLM application?
a: Use prompting alone when the task is within the model's existing knowledge and a good system prompt suffices — zero extra infrastructure. Use RAG when knowledge is private, frequently updated, or needs to be cited and auditable. Use fine-tuning when you need to change how the model behaves — output format, tone, or domain reasoning style — not just what it knows, accepting that it's expensive to update and gives no citations. These aren't mutually exclusive; teams often combine RAG for freshness with fine-tuning for behavior.
fj: I'd fine-tune the model on my documents.
fs: Fine-tuning teaches behavior, not knowledge — and it's expensive to update. For private, frequently-changing documents, RAG is almost always the right first choice.

followups.q: When would you use all three — prompting, RAG, and fine-tuning together?
followups.a: A medical QA bot: fine-tune on medical reasoning style and output format (behavior); RAG over the patient's records and current clinical guidelines (fresh private data); carefully engineered system prompt for safety guardrails and response structure (prompting). Each layer solves a different problem.

q: How do you choose an embedding model for a RAG system?
a: Weigh four axes: retrieval quality on your domain, cost (hosted API vs self-hosted), latency, and dimension size, which affects index storage and search speed. Hosted options like OpenAI text-embedding-3 and Cohere are strong defaults; open-source models like sentence-transformers or BGE let you self-host when privacy or volume-driven cost matters. Crucially, benchmark on your own labeled query-document pairs rather than trusting general MTEB leaderboard rankings, since they don't always transfer to domain-specific content. A pragmatic default is text-embedding-3-small, switching to a self-hosted model if privacy or cost demands it.
fj: I'd use whichever embedding model has the best benchmark score.
fs: I'd benchmark on my own domain data — general MTEB scores don't always transfer. I'd also factor in privacy requirements and volume-driven cost before committing to a hosted API.

followups.q: What is MTEB and why can't you rely on it alone?
followups.a: MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark) is a standardized benchmark for embedding model quality across retrieval, classification, and clustering tasks. It's a useful baseline, but it evaluates on public datasets that may not match your domain. A model that ranks #1 on MTEB may underperform on specialized domain text (legal, medical, code). Always validate on a sample of your actual data.

q: Vector search vs keyword search (BM25) — what does each do well and when would you choose one over the other?
a: Vector (dense) search retrieves by geometric proximity of embeddings, capturing semantic similarity so 'automobile maintenance' matches 'car repair' without shared words. BM25 (sparse) ranks by keyword frequency and wins on exact term matches — product codes, error strings, function names, and rare domain terms that embeddings blur. Vector search wins on paraphrased and conceptual queries; BM25 wins on technical precision. For production, hybrid search — running both and combining scores with Reciprocal Rank Fusion — is usually the safest default because real query distributions include both types.
fj: Vector search is always better because it understands meaning.
fs: Vector search misses exact-term precision — error codes, product names, rare technical terms. For production I default to hybrid search (BM25 + vector + RRF) unless I have evidence that one clearly dominates.

followups.q: What is Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)?
followups.a: RRF combines ranked lists from multiple retrieval systems without requiring score normalization. For each document, compute 1/(k + rank) for each system (k is typically 60), sum across systems, and re-sort. Documents that appear near the top in multiple systems get the highest combined scores. It's parameter-insensitive and works well in practice without training.

q: Explain hybrid search in a RAG pipeline — how does it combine BM25 and dense vector retrieval, and why is it better than either alone?
a: Hybrid search runs two pipelines in parallel — sparse BM25 over an inverted index and dense vector search over the ANN index — then merges and re-ranks the results. BM25 wins on exact tokens like product codes and error strings that embeddings blur into generic regions, while dense retrieval wins on semantic paraphrasing. The standard fusion technique is Reciprocal Rank Fusion, which converts each system's output to rank positions and combines them, avoiding the problem that raw BM25 and cosine scores live on different, non-addable scales. Hybrid is almost always worth it because running two pipelines is cheap and it covers failure modes neither system handles alone.
fj: Dense retrieval understands meaning, so it's always better than BM25.
fs: Dense retrieval loses on exact token precision — product codes, error strings, rare proper nouns. I default to hybrid retrieval to cover both failure modes.

followups.q: How do you tune the balance between BM25 and vector in a hybrid system?
followups.a: Either via the alpha hyperparameter (some systems expose this directly) or by weighting in the RRF formula. Tune by looking at your query distribution — if most queries are technical/exact (product codes, CLI commands), weight BM25 higher; if most are conversational/conceptual, weight vector higher. Evaluate using recall@k on a labeled test set split by query type.

followups.q: What is sparse vector retrieval (SPLADE) and how is it different from BM25?
followups.a: SPLADE learns sparse representations that are still token-based (like BM25) but trained to expand queries with related terms. It combines BM25's precision with some semantic expansion capability. It requires training unlike BM25, but can outperform both pure BM25 and dense retrieval in some settings. It runs on the same inverted-index infrastructure as BM25.

q: Explain cross-encoder reranking in a RAG pipeline — how does it differ from bi-encoder retrieval, and when does it pay off?
a: A bi-encoder encodes query and document into separate vectors, so document embeddings can be pre-computed and searched fast across millions of chunks, but the model never sees the two texts together. A cross-encoder concatenates the query and a candidate document into a single input and scores their relevance directly — far more accurate because it captures fine-grained interactions, but it cannot be pre-computed and must run fresh per pair. The standard design is two-stage: the bi-encoder retrieves top-100 candidates quickly, then the cross-encoder reranks them to top-5, so you never run it on the full corpus. It pays off when top-k precision matters more than the 50-200ms it typically adds.
fj: I'd use a reranker instead of a bi-encoder.
fs: Cross-encoders can't scale to a full corpus — you'd run the reranker on the coarse bi-encoder output, not instead of it. The two-stage architecture exists for exactly this reason.

code.label: retrieve then rerank
lines: pairs = [(query, c.text) for c in cand]

followups.q: How do you decide whether to add a reranker to your pipeline?
followups.a: Run an ablation: measure top-k precision (are the k chunks actually relevant?) before and after the reranker on a labeled test set. If retrieval precision is already high (k=5 chunks are almost always correct), the reranker adds latency with marginal gain. If precision is low — the retriever is pulling noisy results — the reranker fixes the quality problem more efficiently than increasing k or tuning embeddings.

followups.q: What is the 'lost in the middle' problem and how does reranking help?
followups.a: Research shows LLMs underweight information in the middle of long contexts — they attend better to the beginning and end. If you retrieve 10 chunks and the most relevant one lands at position 6, the model may effectively ignore it. Reranking puts the most relevant chunk first, improving the chance the LLM actually uses it. Good reranking + low k often outperforms high k alone.

q: How do you measure retrieval quality in a RAG system? Walk me through recall@k, precision@k, MRR, and NDCG.
a: Recall@k asks whether the ground-truth chunk is present in the top-k results and is the most important RAG metric, because if recall is low the model never sees the right document and no generation improvement helps. Precision@k measures what fraction of retrieved chunks are actually relevant, indicating how much noise enters the prompt. MRR averages the reciprocal rank of the first relevant result and suits single-answer queries but ignores later relevant results, while NDCG rewards higher-ranked relevant results under graded relevance with multiple relevant documents per query. Start by measuring recall@5 and recall@10 on a labeled set of 50-200 query/chunk pairs before optimizing generation.
fj: I'd evaluate RAG quality using BLEU and ROUGE scores.
fs: BLEU/ROUGE measure generation surface similarity, not retrieval quality or factual grounding. For RAG I separate retrieval evaluation (recall@k, precision@k) from generation evaluation (faithfulness, answer relevancy). Evaluating the wrong layer gives you misleading signal.

code.label: recall@k vs precision@k
lines: n = sum(d in rel for d in got)

followups.q: What does it mean if recall@k is high but answer quality is still low?
followups.a: The right chunks are being retrieved (good retriever) but the LLM isn't using them correctly — this is a generation failure, not a retrieval failure. Investigate: are the relevant chunks being injected at the beginning of the prompt? Are there too many irrelevant chunks diluting the signal? Is the LLM ignoring the context and using parametric memory instead? This is why you evaluate retrieval and generation separately.

followups.q: How do you create a ground-truth test set for retrieval evaluation efficiently?
followups.a: Hybrid approach: (1) take 100 real user queries from logs, manually identify which chunks are relevant for each; (2) use an LLM to generate synthetic question-answer pairs from your documents (reverse generation: given a chunk, generate a plausible query), then human-spot-check 20%. This gives you hundreds of labeled pairs quickly. Refresh the test set quarterly as your knowledge base evolves.

q: Walk me through advanced chunking strategies — semantic chunking, sliding window, and parent-child chunking — and when you would use each.
a: Fixed-size with overlap is the simple baseline; sentence- or paragraph-based splitting preserves natural language units for prose. Semantic chunking uses an embedding model to split where topic similarity drops, producing topically coherent chunks at the cost of an embedding call per sentence during ingestion. Sliding window uses heavy overlap to maximize boundary coverage at the expense of index bloat. Parent-child chunking indexes small chunks for retrieval precision but expands a matched chunk to its larger parent before passing to the LLM, decoupling precise retrieval from rich generation context — start simple and escalate based on measured recall and generation quality.
fj: I'd use the default chunking in LangChain.
fs: The default is fixed-size, which is a reasonable start. I'd validate with recall@k on my actual documents and switch to sentence-based, semantic, or parent-child chunking if the retrieval quality is insufficient for the use case.

followups.q: How does LangChain's RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter work and when is it appropriate?
followups.a: It splits on a hierarchy of separators — first tries paragraph breaks, then sentence breaks, then word breaks, then characters — until chunks are under the target size. It preserves natural boundaries when possible, falls back to character splitting when necessary. Appropriate for general-purpose chunking when you want better-than-naive fixed-size splitting without the full cost of semantic chunking.

q: Your RAG system is returning answers that are accurate-sounding but factually wrong. How do you systematically debug whether the problem is in retrieval or generation?
a: Bisect the pipeline and treat retrieval and generation as independent components with independent failure modes. For each failing query, inspect the top-k chunks: if the correct answer isn't in the retrieved context, it's a retrieval failure (fix with better chunking, hybrid search, reranking, or query expansion); if it is present but the answer is wrong, it's a generation failure (fix with faithfulness constraints, lower temperature, or chunk ordering). The decisive diagnostic is the gold-context injection test — manually inject the correct chunk; if the answer becomes correct it's retrieval, if still wrong it's generation. Then quantify with recall@k and faithfulness, and categorize each failure as retrieval, generation, or a genuine knowledge gap.
fj: I'd try a better LLM if the answers are wrong.
fs: First I'd check if the correct chunk was even retrieved — if it wasn't, no LLM upgrade will help. I bisect: inspect retrieved chunks for the failing query, inject gold context to isolate retrieval vs generation failure, then decide which layer to fix.

followups.q: What is the RAGAS framework and which metrics does it provide?
followups.a: RAGAS (Retrieval Augmented Generation Assessment) is an open-source evaluation framework for RAG. Key metrics: Faithfulness (are answer claims supported by context?), Answer Relevancy (does the answer address the query?), Context Precision (what fraction of retrieved context is actually relevant?), Context Recall (does retrieved context contain the needed information?). It can score without human ground-truth answers by using LLM judges, making it practical for automated evaluation pipelines.

followups.q: What is 'context relevance' vs 'faithfulness' in RAG evaluation?
followups.a: Context relevance (or precision) measures the retriever: are the retrieved chunks relevant to the query? Faithfulness measures the generator: are the model's claims grounded in the retrieved context? A system can have high context relevance but low faithfulness (retrieved the right docs, LLM still hallucinated) or low context relevance but high faithfulness (retrieved wrong docs, LLM faithfully followed wrong context and produced a wrong answer). Distinguishing them is exactly the retrieval-vs-generation bisection.

q: How do you add metadata filtering to a RAG retrieval pipeline, and when is it worth the added complexity?
a: Metadata filtering pre-filters candidate chunks by structured attributes — date, document type, author, department — before or alongside vector similarity, so pure similarity doesn't return semantically close but organizationally irrelevant chunks. You store metadata alongside each chunk and add a where clause at query time. Pre-filtering restricts the search before ANN (fast, required for strict access control) while post-filtering retrieves by similarity then filters (preserves recall but may return fewer than k). It's worth it when users have natural access-control boundaries, when similar document types must stay organizationally distinct, or when freshness matters; skip it when all documents share the same type and time range.
fj: I'd just do pure vector search and let similarity handle relevance.
fs: Pure similarity ignores organizational scope — a user's query might be semantically similar to chunks from a department or time period that should be excluded. Metadata filtering is how you encode business logic into retrieval.

followups.q: How do you extract and store metadata from unstructured documents?
followups.a: For structured sources (CRMs, databases), metadata is attached natively. For unstructured documents (PDFs, docs), use parsing libraries (PyMuPDF, Unstructured.io) to extract headers, dates, authors, and section titles. For documents without explicit metadata, use an LLM to extract or classify attributes during ingestion (e.g., classify document type, extract date from text). Store metadata in the vector database's metadata field alongside the chunk embedding.

q: RAG vs long-context LLMs — now that models have 128K–1M token context windows, is RAG still necessary?
a: Long context puts all documents directly in the prompt, which works when the corpus is small, stable, and latency-tolerant. But several RAG advantages survive: cost (RAG injects a few thousand tokens vs millions per query), latency, immediate knowledge-base updates via re-indexing, and recall — LLMs demonstrably underweight information in the middle of long contexts, the 'lost in the middle' problem, whereas RAG puts the right chunks first. RAG also provides natural citation and auditability. Long context wins for small corpora needing whole-corpus reasoning, so the two are complementary: RAG at scale, long context for small, complex, high-value queries.
fj: Now that models have 1M context, RAG is obsolete.
fs: Long context doesn't fix cost at scale, the lost-in-the-middle recall problem, or knowledge base update latency. RAG is still the right default for large dynamic corpora — long context is complementary for small-corpus, high-value, complex-reasoning queries.

followups.q: What is the 'lost in the middle' problem and what evidence exists for it?
followups.a: Liu et al. (2023, Stanford) showed that LLM performance on multi-document QA degrades significantly when the relevant document is in the middle of the context, compared to the beginning or end. Models exhibit a U-shaped performance curve by document position. This is well-replicated and affects GPT-4, Claude, and most frontier models, though newer models with better long-context training have partially mitigated it.

q: Your production RAG system gives answers that are plausible-sounding but factually wrong. Describe your end-to-end debugging methodology.
a: Don't start with the LLM — the common mistake is blaming generation when retrieval is the culprit. Build a failure sample of 20-50 definitively wrong queries tagged with the correct answer and source document, then bisect at the retrieval boundary: if the ground-truth chunk isn't in top-k it's a retrieval failure (invest in hybrid search, reranking, query expansion, chunking), and if it is present but the answer is wrong it's a generation failure (invest in faithfulness constraints, temperature, chunk ordering). The gold-context injection test settles it: inject the correct chunk and see if the answer corrects. Then quantify recall@k, faithfulness, and accuracy at scale, and slice failures into retrieval, generation, and genuine knowledge-gap categories, each with a different fix.
fj: I'd try a bigger/better LLM model.
fs: Upgrading the LLM won't help if the right context was never retrieved. I bisect first — inspect the retrieved chunks for each failure. If the correct chunk isn't in top-k, it's a retrieval problem no model upgrade can fix.

followups.q: How do you automate faithfulness checking at scale in production?
followups.a: Two approaches: (1) LLM-as-judge — for each response, prompt a secondary LLM (smaller, cheaper) with 'given this context, does the following claim appear in the context or contradict it?' and aggregate a faithfulness rate. (2) NLI (natural language inference) classifier — run an NLI model on context/claim pairs to detect entailment vs contradiction vs neutral. LLM-as-judge is more flexible but more expensive; NLI is faster and cheaper for high-volume use. Both should be monitored for drift as your corpus and query distribution evolve.

followups.q: What is 'context stuffing' and how does it relate to generation failures?
followups.a: Context stuffing is injecting a large number of retrieved chunks into the prompt, hoping quantity compensates for quality. It typically makes generation worse: the model gets distracted by irrelevant chunks, the most relevant chunk gets 'lost in the middle,' and the model may hallucinate by blending conflicting chunks. The fix is aggressive chunk quality control — use a reranker, limit to top-3–5 high-quality chunks, and measure faithfulness rather than just recall.

followups.q: How do you monitor RAG quality continuously in production without labeled data for every query?
followups.a: Multiple signals: (1) Retrieval similarity score distribution — if average similarity scores drop, your queries are drifting away from your corpus. (2) LLM-as-judge faithfulness on a sample of production responses (automated). (3) User feedback signals (thumbs up/down, follow-up clarification questions as implicit negative signals). (4) Semantic drift in query embeddings — if the cluster structure of user queries shifts, your knowledge base coverage may be degrading. Alert on all of these with threshold-based monitoring.

q: Design a retrieval evaluation harness for a production RAG system. What components does it need and how do you keep it current?
a: The harness needs a labeled query-document test set, retrieval metrics (recall@k, precision@k, MRR, NDCG), generation-quality metrics (faithfulness, answer relevancy), an automated CI gate, and continuous production monitoring. Build the test set from 100-500 real user queries with human-plus-LLM-assisted labeling, including edge cases like no-answer, multi-hop, and conflicting-information queries. The CI gate runs the full harness on every change to the knowledge base, embedding model, chunking, or pipeline, and fails the deploy if recall@5 drops more than 5% from baseline. In production, sample a few percent of traffic for LLM-as-judge faithfulness and track score and query-cluster drift, refreshing the test set quarterly as the corpus and user distribution evolve.
fj: I'd evaluate by trying a few queries manually.
fs: Manual evaluation doesn't scale and misses silent regressions. I'd build a labeled test set, compute recall@k and faithfulness automatically, and gate deploys on these metrics — just like a unit test suite for the retrieval pipeline.

followups.q: How do you prevent your evaluation harness from having circular evaluation (using the same LLM to evaluate itself)?
followups.a: Two strategies: (1) use a different model family as the judge — if your RAG pipeline uses GPT-4o for generation, use Claude Sonnet or Gemini as the judge. Different training distributions reduce correlated failure. (2) Calibrate the LLM judge against human labels on a subset — measure judge accuracy relative to human ground truth and discard the judge if correlation is below a threshold (typically r > 0.75). Never rely solely on LLM-as-judge without this calibration step.

followups.q: How do you handle the test set coverage problem — users ask questions you didn't anticipate?
followups.a: Continuously mine production query logs for novel query types that cluster away from your existing test set coverage. Use embedding clustering to identify uncovered regions in query space. When a new cluster appears (e.g., users start asking about a new feature that wasn't in the original knowledge base), add that cluster to the test set and the knowledge base. Think of it as a feedback loop: production distribution informs test set coverage, test set coverage informs evaluation completeness.

q: Walk me through how you would design a RAG system for a large enterprise with 10 million documents, strict access control, and a requirement for cited, grounded answers.
a: Ingest with semantic chunking and rich metadata (document ID, section, owner, access labels, timestamp), embed with a high-quality model in batches, and build a hybrid index of dense HNSW vectors plus a BM25 inverted index sharded by access domain. At query time, pre-filter by the user's access labels (pre-filter, never post-filter, for security), retrieve top-100 hybrid candidates, rerank with a cross-encoder to top-5, and deduplicate. Generate with a strict prompt that answers only from context and emits citations per claim, then run a post-generation faithfulness check via NLI or an LLM judge to verify each claim is actually supported. Log every query, retrieved chunks, answer, faithfulness score, and latency, and alert on faithfulness drops and similarity drift.
fj: I'd use a simple vector search with a post-filter for access control.
fs: Post-filtering retrieves from the full index before applying permissions — that's a security gap and a performance problem at 10M docs. Access control must be a pre-filter enforced at the index level, before similarity scoring.

followups.q: How would you handle document updates in a 10M-document index?
followups.a: Incremental updates using a document change detection pipeline: detect changed/new/deleted documents via checksum or timestamp, delete the old chunks for changed documents (by document ID), re-embed and re-insert the new chunks. Queue these as background jobs. The index is eventually consistent — documents are stale for the duration of the update pipeline (typically minutes to hours depending on throughput). For critical documents with strict freshness requirements, implement a push-invalidation mechanism that marks specific document chunks as stale and forces re-retrieval from the source.

followups.q: How do you handle the case where two retrieved chunks contain conflicting information?
followups.a: Explicitly: add a conflict detection step in the prompt — 'if the provided documents contain conflicting information, acknowledge the conflict in your answer and cite which sources say what.' For high-stakes use cases (legal, medical), surface the conflict to the user rather than having the model silently pick one. Log these conflicts for knowledge base quality review — conflicting documents often indicate that one is outdated and needs to be updated or deprecated.

q: What is faithfulness in RAG evaluation, how do you measure it, and what do you do when faithfulness is high but answers are still wrong?
a: Faithfulness measures whether every factual claim in the generated answer is supported by the retrieved context — a faithful answer never introduces facts absent from the chunks, even correct ones from parametric memory. Measure it with RAGAS, which decomposes the answer into claims and checks each against context, or with a cheaper NLI entailment classifier at scale. High faithfulness but wrong answers means the model is faithfully following context that is itself wrong, outdated, or incomplete — a knowledge-base problem, not a generation one. The fix is upstream: audit the retrieved chunks, correct or deprecate bad source documents, add freshness metadata, and check retrieval precision; only low faithfulness calls for prompt and generation fixes.
fj: If faithfulness is high, the system is working correctly.
fs: High faithfulness only means the model followed the retrieved context. If the context itself was wrong or outdated, faithfulness is high but answers are still incorrect. Faithfulness measures generation grounding, not answer accuracy — you need both.

code.label: RAGAS faithfulness
lines: # high score + wrong = bad source doc

followups.q: How would you build an automated pipeline to detect and flag stale or incorrect knowledge base documents?
followups.a: Multi-signal approach: (1) timestamp + freshness policy — automatically flag documents older than a domain-specific threshold (e.g., 90 days for product documentation, 1 year for stable technical references); (2) conflict detection — when two documents make contradictory claims about the same entity, flag both for human review; (3) downstream signal — when answers based on a specific document consistently fail faithfulness checks or get negative user feedback, flag that document for quality review. Build a document quality score that aggregates these signals and surfaces low-quality documents for editorial review.

q: How do you decide between vector-only retrieval, hybrid retrieval, and adding a reranker? Walk me through your decision process and the signals you look at.
a: Start with BM25 alone as a free baseline that any dense or hybrid system should beat on your real query distribution. Choose dense vector-only for semantic, conversational queries; choose hybrid (BM25 + dense + RRF) as the safe default for mixed distributions since it covers both failure modes with little added cost. Add a reranker only when recall@k is adequate but precision@k is poor, because a reranker reorders what was already retrieved and cannot fix low recall. The signals: low recall@k means fix retrieval upstream, high recall with low precision means add a reranker, and high recall and precision with poor answers means a generation problem — while always watching your latency budget.
fj: I'd add a reranker to improve RAG quality.
fs: A reranker improves precision within retrieved candidates but can't fix low recall — it reorders what's already there. I'd check recall@k first: if recall is low, the reranker won't help, and I need to fix retrieval upstream.

followups.q: When would you NOT use hybrid retrieval despite its general advantages?
followups.a: When the query distribution is demonstrably uniform — for example, a code search system where all queries are exact function or class names, or a medical ICD code lookup where all queries are codes. In those cases, BM25 alone may achieve near-perfect recall and the added complexity of dense retrieval has negative ROI. Measure first; don't add hybrid as a reflex.

followups.q: How do you select the reranker model to use?
followups.a: Evaluate on your domain: benchmark Cohere Rerank, BGE-Reranker, cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM variants on a sample of your query-document pairs. Measure both NDCG improvement over the bi-encoder ranking and latency. Cohere Rerank is lowest operational overhead (hosted API) but adds cost and a network hop. Self-hosted BGE-Reranker is cheaper at scale but requires inference infrastructure. Pick the smallest model that crosses your quality threshold — reranker latency scales with model size.

q: How do you scale a RAG system from prototype to production — what changes and what breaks?
a: The prototype validates capability; production forces you to engineer operational concerns. Embedding moves from synchronous per-query calls to caching plus async batch ingestion, and ANN index memory becomes real — millions of high-dim vectors need quantization, dimensionality reduction, or domain sharding. Knowledge-base freshness shifts from full re-indexing to incremental update pipelines, access control becomes per-tenant enforcement at the index level, and evaluation moves from ad-hoc testing to automated recall@k CI gates plus production faithfulness monitoring. You also add cost governance (caching, smaller rerankers, batching) and must profile the full embed-retrieve-rerank-generate path against P95 latency SLAs.
fj: I'd just scale up the vector database.
fs: Scaling the database is necessary but not sufficient — the production gaps are in knowledge base freshness pipelines, embedding cost and latency at scale, access control enforcement, and automated evaluation. I address each of these explicitly.

followups.q: How do you reduce embedding costs in production?
followups.a: Multiple levers: (1) exact-match query cache — identical queries (common in enterprise use) hit cache, no embedding call needed; (2) smaller embedding models — validate quality on your domain first, but models like text-embedding-3-small are 5x cheaper than text-embedding-3-large with similar quality for most corpora; (3) batch ingestion — embed documents in large batches during off-peak hours rather than per-document real-time; (4) caching at ingestion — never re-embed unchanged documents, detect changes by content hash.

q: Systematically compare vector-only retrieval vs hybrid retrieval (BM25 + vector) — when does hybrid actually move the needle and when is it not worth the complexity?
a: Vector-only wins when queries are purely semantic, query and document vocabulary align, and the embedding model fits the domain — well-written prose knowledge bases are strong candidates. Hybrid wins on mixed query distributions, which nearly all real corpora have, and on multilingual corpora where BM25's language-agnosticism complements English-trained dense models. The empirical check is to run BM25 alone: high recall@5 means strong keyword signal where hybrid will likely outperform dense, while very low BM25 recall means highly paraphrastic queries where hybrid adds marginal benefit. Modern vector databases support hybrid natively so complexity is low, but skip it for token-precise domains like code or medical-code lookup where BM25 alone often dominates.
fj: I'd always use hybrid retrieval since it's always better.
fs: Hybrid is generally better on mixed corpora, but it adds infrastructure complexity and isn't always necessary. I'd run BM25 alone as a baseline first — if it already achieves high recall@5, the ROI on hybrid may not justify the added complexity for that specific corpus.

followups.q: How does sparse vector retrieval (SPLADE, BM25++) compare to traditional BM25 and dense retrieval?
followups.a: SPLADE learns sparse representations that include implicit term expansion — if a document contains 'automobile', SPLADE's representation may also activate 'car' and 'vehicle' tokens, giving it semantic expansion while remaining sparse (and therefore indexable by an inverted index). BM25 can only match exact or stemmed terms; SPLADE partially closes the vocabulary gap. Dense retrieval still outperforms SPLADE on extreme paraphrase, but SPLADE beats BM25 significantly on recall for in-domain corpora. It requires training, unlike BM25, and runs on standard inverted-index infrastructure.

q: What is query rewriting in RAG, and why does the raw user query often retrieve poorly?
a: Query rewriting transforms the user's raw input into one or more retrieval-optimized queries before embedding or BM25 search. Raw queries retrieve poorly because they are often short, ambiguous, full of pronouns from a multi-turn chat, or phrased differently from the document vocabulary — an embedding of 'why is it slow?' carries almost no signal. Common rewrites are: resolving conversational references into a standalone query, expanding with synonyms and domain terms, and splitting a compound question into sub-queries. The key rule is to preserve the user's original intent — rewriting should add context, not change the question.
fj: I just embed whatever the user typed and search.
fs: Short or conversational queries embed poorly — I rewrite into a standalone, vocabulary-aligned query first, while preserving the original intent. That single step often lifts recall more than tuning the index.

code.label: standalone rewrite
lines: # turn chat turn into standalone q

followups.q: How do you stop query rewriting from drifting away from the user's actual intent?
followups.a: Constrain the rewrite prompt to 'add context and disambiguate, never change the question,' and keep the original query in the retrieval mix (e.g., run both raw and rewritten, union via RRF). On ambiguous inputs, prefer multi-query expansion over a single confident rewrite so a wrong guess doesn't replace the right one. Log rewrite/original pairs and spot-check for intent drift.

followups.q: In a multi-turn chat, how do you resolve pronouns and references for retrieval?
followups.a: Run a dedicated coreference/standalone-rewrite step that folds the relevant conversation history into a self-contained query — 'why is it slow?' becomes 'why is the nightly Spark ingestion job slow?'. Pass only the salient prior turns, not the whole transcript, to avoid diluting the query. The embedded text must stand alone because the index has no memory of the dialogue.

followups.q: What's the latency cost of query rewriting and when do you skip it?
followups.a: Each rewrite adds an LLM round-trip (often 200-600ms) before retrieval even starts. Skip it for queries that are already standalone and vocabulary-aligned — a cheap classifier or length/heuristic gate can route only short, conversational, or ambiguous queries through the rewriter. For latency-critical paths, use a small fast model for the rewrite rather than the main generation model.

q: Explain HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) and multi-query retrieval. When does each help and what's the cost?
a: HyDE asks an LLM to write a hypothetical answer to the query, then embeds that fake answer instead of the question — because an answer shares vocabulary and structure with real documents, it lands closer to the right region of embedding space, which helps most in zero-shot domains with no labeled data. Multi-query retrieval generates several paraphrases or sub-questions, retrieves for each, and unions the results (deduping via RRF), widening recall for ambiguous or compound queries. Both trade extra LLM calls and latency for recall, and HyDE can hallucinate a misleading pseudo-document on niche topics. A query router that classifies each query and only triggers these when needed keeps cost bounded.
fj: HyDE just means generating a better-worded query.
fs: HyDE embeds a hypothetical answer, not a reworded question — answers sit closer to real documents in embedding space. I gate it behind a router since it adds an LLM call and can hallucinate on niche topics.

followups.q: When does HyDE actively hurt retrieval?
followups.a: On niche or factual queries the LLM has no real knowledge of, it writes a confident but wrong hypothetical document, and you embed that fabrication — pulling toward the wrong region of embedding space. HyDE helps in zero-shot semantic domains but is risky for rare entities or fresh facts. Gate it behind a router and fall back to the raw query on low-confidence topics.

followups.q: How do you combine multiple retrieved sets from multi-query without blowing up the prompt?
followups.a: Dedup across the unioned results and fuse with Reciprocal Rank Fusion so chunks appearing in several sub-query results rank highest, then truncate to a small top-k (3-5) and optionally rerank. The point of multi-query is recall breadth, but you still pass a tight context to generation — never dump every sub-query's full top-k into the prompt or you trigger lost-in-the-middle.

followups.q: Can you use HyDE and multi-query together?
followups.a: Yes — generate several query paraphrases, write a hypothetical document for each, embed and retrieve per HyDE-doc, then fuse with RRF. It maximizes recall on hard, ambiguous queries but multiplies LLM calls and latency. Reserve the combination for high-value queries flagged by a router; for routine lookups the cost rarely justifies the marginal recall gain.

q: How do you make a RAG system produce inline citations, and how do you verify a citation actually supports the sentence it's attached to?
a: Give each retrieved chunk a stable ID, instruct the model to answer only from context and tag each sentence with the IDs it used, and parse those tags into clickable references back to the source span. Generation-time citations alone aren't trustworthy because the model can attach a plausible-looking ID that doesn't actually support the claim. So you add a post-hoc verification pass: for each (sentence, cited chunk) pair, run an NLI entailment check or LLM judge and flag sentences whose cited text doesn't entail them. This is the difference between a citation that exists and a citation that is correct — fine-grained, sentence-level attribution with verification is the 2025 standard.
fj: I tell the model to add a [1] after each sentence and trust it.
fs: A model-emitted citation can point at a chunk that doesn't support the claim. I assign stable chunk IDs, then verify each sentence-citation pair with an NLI entailment check before trusting it.

followups.q: What do you do with a sentence whose citation fails the entailment check?
followups.a: Treat it as an unsupported claim: either suppress the sentence, regenerate with a stricter 'answer only from context' instruction, or re-retrieve for that specific claim and re-attribute. Surfacing an unverified citation is worse than no citation — it manufactures false trust. In high-stakes domains, route flagged sentences to human review rather than auto-publishing.

followups.q: Why use NLI entailment instead of just checking string overlap with the cited chunk?
followups.a: String overlap misses paraphrase — a faithful answer often restates the source in different words, so lexical matching produces false negatives, while a hallucinated sentence can share words with a chunk that doesn't actually support it (false positive). NLI judges semantic entailment: does the chunk logically support the claim? That's the property citations need, and overlap can't measure it.

followups.q: How do you get the model to cite at the sentence level rather than dumping all chunk IDs at the end?
followups.a: Instruct it to emit the supporting chunk ID(s) immediately after each sentence or claim, and give a few-shot example of that exact format. Coarse end-of-answer citations are unverifiable — you can't map a specific claim to a specific source. Sentence-level tags are what make the per-pair NLI verification possible; enforce the format with structured output if the model drifts.

q: Your RAG answers are grounded and the right chunk is clearly in the context, yet the model still misses the answer or formats it wrong. Which failure points are these and how do you fix them?
a: These are the generation-side failure points from the 'Seven Failure Points' taxonomy: 'not extracted' (the answer is in context but the LLM fails to pull it out, often drowned by noise or buried mid-context), 'wrong format' (it ignores formatting instructions), and 'incorrect specificity' (answer too broad or too narrow). Fixes are different from retrieval fixes: cut k and rerank so the right chunk sits near the top (countering lost-in-the-middle), tighten the prompt with explicit extraction and format instructions, lower temperature, and add a few-shot example of the exact output shape. If formatting must be exact, enforce it with structured/JSON output rather than hoping the model complies.
fj: The chunk is there, so it must be a retrieval problem.
fs: Right chunk in context but wrong answer is a generation failure — 'not extracted' or 'wrong format', not retrieval. I rerank to fight lost-in-the-middle, tighten extraction instructions, and enforce format with structured output.

followups.q: What are the Seven Failure Points and how do they split across retrieval and generation?
followups.a: From Barnett et al.'s taxonomy: retrieval-side are 'missing content' (not in corpus), 'missed top-k,' and 'not in context' (retrieved but dropped during consolidation); generation-side are 'not extracted,' 'wrong format,' 'incorrect specificity,' and 'incomplete.' Mapping a failure to its point tells you which layer to fix — the whole value is not blaming generation for a retrieval miss.

followups.q: Why does lowering temperature help 'not extracted' failures?
followups.a: Lower temperature makes the model more deterministic and literal, so it's likelier to lift the exact fact from context rather than paraphrase, embellish, or wander into parametric memory. Extraction-style answers want fidelity, not creativity — temperature near 0 reduces the chance the model 'improves' the answer away from the source. It won't fix a buried-mid-context chunk; reorder for that.

followups.q: When should you enforce structured output instead of just instructing the format in the prompt?
followups.a: When the format is load-bearing for a downstream consumer — JSON for an API, a fixed schema for a UI, exact field names. Prompt instructions are best-effort and the model occasionally drifts; constrained/JSON-mode decoding or a schema validator guarantees the shape or fails loudly. Reserve plain prompt instructions for soft formatting where an occasional deviation is harmless.

q: In hybrid search, why can't you just add BM25 and cosine scores together, and how does Reciprocal Rank Fusion solve it versus a weighted convex combination?
a: BM25 scores are unbounded and corpus-dependent while cosine similarity sits in roughly [-1, 1], so summing them lets one system dominate purely because of scale, not relevance — naive score addition is broken. Reciprocal Rank Fusion sidesteps this by discarding raw scores and combining rank positions: each document gets sum of 1/(k+rank) across systems (k≈60), so only ordering matters and the scales never need normalizing. The alternative, a convex combination, min-max normalizes each system's scores then blends them as alpha*dense + (1-alpha)*sparse; it can edge out RRF when tuned because it preserves score magnitude, but it requires per-corpus normalization and tuning alpha. RRF is the robust zero-tuning default; convex combination is the option you reach for when you can afford to tune.
fj: I normalize both scores to 0-1 and average them.
fs: BM25 and cosine live on different, non-comparable scales, so adding them lets scale—not relevance—decide. RRF fuses by rank and needs no normalization; a tuned convex combination can beat it but requires per-corpus normalization and an alpha sweep.

code.label: convex combo (tunable)
lines: # a in [0,1]; RRF avoids this tuning

followups.q: What does the k constant in RRF (typically 60) actually control?
followups.a: It dampens the influence of top ranks: 1/(k+rank) means with k=60 the gap between rank 1 and rank 2 is small, so no single system's #1 result dominates the fusion. A smaller k sharpens the emphasis on top results; a larger k flattens the contribution across ranks. 60 is the empirical default from the original RRF paper and rarely needs tuning, which is exactly why RRF is the zero-tuning choice.

followups.q: When is a tuned convex combination worth it over RRF?
followups.a: When you have a labeled eval set, a stable corpus, and can measure that preserving score magnitude (not just rank) lifts quality — e.g., one system's high-confidence top hit should outweigh another's marginal results. The cost is per-corpus min-max normalization plus an alpha sweep re-tuned as the corpus drifts. RRF is the safe default; convex combination squeezes the last few points.

followups.q: Does RRF's rank-only approach ever lose useful information?
followups.a: Yes — by discarding scores it ignores confidence magnitude. If BM25 returns one document with a far higher score than the rest (a near-exact match), RRF treats it as just 'rank 1,' on par with a weak dense rank-1. Where score gaps carry strong signal, a normalized convex combination captures that; RRF's robustness comes precisely from throwing the signal away.

q: What is Anthropic's Contextual Retrieval, and what specific failure does prepending context to each chunk fix?
a: Contextual Retrieval fixes the problem that an isolated chunk often loses the context that makes it findable — a chunk saying 'revenue grew 3%' has no idea which company or quarter it refers to. Before embedding and BM25-indexing each chunk, you ask an LLM to write a short 50-100 token blurb situating that chunk within its parent document, then prepend it. You index the contextualized chunk for both the dense vector and the BM25 inverted index. Anthropic reported this cuts retrieval failure substantially, and pairing it with a reranker compounds the gain. Prompt caching the document makes the per-chunk LLM call cheap enough for production ingestion.
fj: Contextual Retrieval is just adding more overlap between chunks.
fs: Overlap copies neighboring text; Contextual Retrieval generates an LLM summary that situates the chunk in the whole document, then indexes that for both vector and BM25 — it restores global context a fixed window can't.

code.label: contextualize before index
lines: ctx = llm('Situate this chunk in the',

followups.q: Doesn't generating a context blurb per chunk make ingestion prohibitively expensive?
followups.a: It would, except you prompt-cache the parent document: the document is written to cache once, then each per-chunk call only pays for the small chunk plus the cached prefix at a heavy discount. Anthropic's recipe relies on this — it's what makes contextualizing every chunk economical at corpus scale. Without prompt caching, the per-chunk LLM calls would dominate the ingestion bill.

followups.q: Why prepend the context to the BM25 index too, not just the vector index?
followups.a: Because the contextual blurb adds the disambiguating tokens — company name, quarter, section — that a keyword search needs to match. A bare chunk saying 'revenue grew 3%' has no 'Acme Q3' tokens for BM25 to hit. Indexing the enriched text in both the dense and sparse indices is what lets Contextual Retrieval improve hybrid recall, not just semantic recall.

followups.q: How does Contextual Retrieval compare to parent-child chunking?
followups.a: Parent-child retrieves on a small chunk then expands to the parent at generation time — it fixes generation context, not retrieval matching. Contextual Retrieval bakes situating context into the indexed text, so the small chunk becomes findable in the first place. They're complementary: contextualize for retrieval recall, expand to parent for richer generation context.

q: How does embedding dimensionality affect a RAG system, and what are Matryoshka embeddings?
a: Dimensionality sets the storage and search cost: every vector in the index is N floats, so 3072 dims costs roughly 4x the RAM and ANN compute of 768 dims, which dominates the bill at tens of millions of chunks. Matryoshka Representation Learning trains the model so the most important information is front-loaded into the early dimensions, letting you truncate a 1536-dim vector to 256 dims and keep most of the quality. The standard pattern is a coarse-to-fine search: shortlist candidates with truncated low-dim vectors, then re-score the shortlist with the full vectors. Combine truncation with scalar or binary quantization for further memory savings, validating recall@k at each compression step rather than assuming it's free.
fj: Higher embedding dimensions always mean better retrieval.
fs: More dimensions raise storage and search cost with diminishing quality returns. Matryoshka models let me truncate to 256-512 dims, shortlist cheaply, then re-rank with full vectors — I validate recall@k at each step.

followups.q: How is Matryoshka truncation different from running PCA or dimensionality reduction after the fact?
followups.a: Matryoshka is trained so early dimensions are intentionally the most informative — you truncate by slicing the vector, with no projection step and no fitted transform. PCA learns a projection post-hoc that must be stored and refit as data drifts. With Matryoshka the same model emits a 256-dim or 1536-dim view of one vector; you take a prefix, which makes coarse-to-fine search essentially free.

followups.q: Can you combine Matryoshka truncation with quantization, and in what order?
followups.a: Yes — they're orthogonal compressions. Typically truncate dimensions first (fewer floats per vector), then quantize each remaining float to int8 or binary for further RAM savings. Stack them on the shortlist stage, then re-score survivors with full-precision, full-dimension vectors. Validate recall@k after each step, since the losses compound and the safe budget is corpus-dependent.

followups.q: What's the failure mode if you truncate too aggressively?
followups.a: Recall collapses: at very low dimensions, distinct chunks crowd into the same region and the coarse shortlist starts excluding the true positive, which the full-vector re-score can never recover — it only re-ranks what the shortlist returned. The fix is to keep the shortlist k generous (e.g., 200) at low dimensions and validate recall@k at each truncation level.

q: Compare recursive character splitting, semantic chunking, and Markdown/structure-aware chunking. When is each the right call?
a: Recursive character splitting tries a priority list of separators — paragraphs, then lines, then sentences, then characters — so it keeps natural boundaries until it must hard-cut to fit the size limit; it's the robust default for messy prose. Semantic chunking embeds each sentence and starts a new chunk where adjacent similarity drops below a threshold, producing topically coherent chunks at the cost of an embedding call per sentence at ingestion. Structure-aware chunking splits on the document's own markup — Markdown headers, HTML sections, code blocks — which beats both for well-structured docs like API references or wikis because the author already marked the topic boundaries. Match the strategy to the document's structure rather than picking one globally.
fj: Semantic chunking is the most advanced, so I'd always use it.
fs: Semantic chunking costs an embedding per sentence and isn't always best. For structured docs I split on the existing headers; for messy prose recursive splitting is the robust default. I match the splitter to the document shape.

followups.q: What threshold do you use to decide a semantic-chunk boundary, and how do you set it?
followups.a: You compute embedding similarity between adjacent sentences and cut where it drops below a threshold — often a percentile of the similarity-gap distribution (e.g., 95th-percentile breakpoint) rather than a fixed cosine value. Per-document percentiles adapt to varying writing styles. Tune by checking that chunks stay topically coherent and that recall@k holds on your eval set.

followups.q: How do you chunk a document that mixes prose and code blocks?
followups.a: Structure-aware splitting: never break inside a code block — treat fenced code as an atomic unit (or split on function/class boundaries), and chunk the surrounding prose separately, attaching the code's heading as metadata. Recursive character splitting can shred a code block mid-function and destroy its meaning, so the markup boundaries take priority over the token-size target for code.

followups.q: Why not just always use structure-aware chunking since it respects author intent?
followups.a: Because it depends on reliable structure that many documents don't have — OCR'd PDFs, scraped HTML with broken markup, or plain-text dumps with no headers. When structure is absent or noisy, header-splitting produces garbage boundaries, and recursive character splitting is the robust fallback. You match the splitter to whether trustworthy structure actually exists in the source.

q: What is ColBERT / late-interaction retrieval, and what does it buy you over a single-vector bi-encoder?
a: A single-vector bi-encoder compresses a whole chunk into one embedding, forcing all meaning through a single dot product. ColBERT keeps a separate vector per token and scores a query-document pair with MaxSim — for each query token it takes the max similarity over all document tokens, then sums. This token-level matching gives much better out-of-domain and long-context retrieval, and small ColBERT models can beat far larger single-vector models on hard benchmarks. The cost is storage and latency: you store many small vectors per chunk and the MaxSim scoring is heavier, so teams reserve it for high-value corpora or use it as a reranking stage over a cheap first-pass retriever rather than over the whole index.
fj: ColBERT is just a bigger, better embedding model.
fs: ColBERT keeps per-token vectors and scores with MaxSim instead of one pooled dot product — that's why it generalizes out-of-domain. The price is much larger storage and latency, so I often use it as a reranker, not over the full index.

code.label: MaxSim late interaction
lines: # each q token -> best d token,

followups.q: Why does ColBERT generalize better out-of-domain than a single-vector bi-encoder?
followups.a: Pooling a whole chunk into one vector forces a lossy compromise — rare or domain-specific terms get averaged away. ColBERT keeps a vector per token, so a critical out-of-domain term (a product code, a rare entity) can still match its counterpart via MaxSim even when the overall topic is unfamiliar. Token-level matching degrades more gracefully under query-distribution shift.

followups.q: How do you control ColBERT's storage blow-up at scale?
followups.a: Each chunk stores one vector per token, so indexes balloon. Mitigations: aggressive vector quantization (ColBERTv2 uses residual/centroid compression), capping tokens per document, and pruning low-information tokens. The common pattern is to use ColBERT only as a reranking stage over a first-pass retriever's top candidates, scoring per-token vectors for a small set rather than the corpus.

followups.q: When is a single-vector bi-encoder still the better choice?
followups.a: When the corpus is large, latency and storage budgets are tight, and queries stay in-domain — a well-fit bi-encoder is far cheaper to index and search, and ANN over single vectors is highly optimized. ColBERT's accuracy gains often don't justify multiplying your index size and scoring cost. Reach for late interaction on high-value, hard, or out-of-domain corpora, not routine semantic lookup.

q: What is GraphRAG, and what kind of question does it answer that vanilla vector RAG cannot?
a: GraphRAG builds a knowledge graph from the corpus — an LLM extracts entities and relationships, then community detection clusters them and summarizes each cluster. It targets global, sense-making questions like 'what are the main themes across these 10,000 reports?', which vanilla top-k RAG fails because no single chunk contains the answer and similarity search can't aggregate across the whole corpus. GraphRAG offers local search (traverse a specific entity's neighborhood for factual queries) and global search (synthesize community summaries for corpus-wide questions). The trade-off is a heavy, expensive LLM-driven indexing pass and graph maintenance, so it's justified for connected, entity-rich corpora needing multi-hop or thematic reasoning — not for simple lookup over independent documents.
fj: GraphRAG is just RAG with a fancier vector database.
fs: GraphRAG extracts an entity-relationship graph and pre-summarizes communities, so it can answer corpus-wide thematic and multi-hop questions vector RAG can't — at the cost of an expensive LLM indexing pass. I use it only for entity-rich, connected corpora.

followups.q: What's the difference between GraphRAG local search and global search?
followups.a: Local search answers entity-specific questions by traversing a node's neighborhood — 'what contracts does vendor X have?' — pulling connected entities and their chunks. Global search answers corpus-wide thematic questions — 'main risk themes?' — by map-reducing over precomputed community summaries. Local is cheap and targeted; global trades cost for whole-corpus synthesis.

followups.q: What makes GraphRAG indexing so expensive, and how do you keep it current?
followups.a: Indexing runs an LLM over the whole corpus to extract entities and relationships, then more LLM calls to summarize each community — many calls scaling with corpus size, far costlier than embedding. Keeping it fresh is hard because adding documents can reshape community structure; teams batch re-index periodically rather than per-document, accepting graph staleness as the trade-off.

followups.q: When is GraphRAG the wrong tool despite a question being 'complex'?
followups.a: When documents are independent and the answer lives in one place — a complex but single-source lookup (a detailed policy clause) is better served by vector RAG plus reranking. GraphRAG only pays off when the answer requires connecting facts across many entities or documents (multi-hop, thematic aggregation). For an unconnected corpus it's expensive overhead with no retrieval benefit.

q: Explain agentic RAG and the Corrective RAG (CRAG) and Self-RAG patterns. What problem with naive RAG do they solve?
a: Naive RAG retrieves exactly once and generates, so if that single retrieval is poor the answer is doomed. Agentic RAG wraps retrieval in a reasoning loop that decides whether to retrieve, what to retrieve, and when to stop. Corrective RAG (CRAG) adds a lightweight evaluator that grades the retrieved docs; if they're weak it triggers a corrective path — rewrite the query, retrieve again, or fall back to web search — before generating. Self-RAG trains the model to emit reflection tokens that judge relevance and whether each sentence is supported, retrieving on demand mid-generation. Both shine on multi-hop and ambiguous queries where one-shot retrieval misses; the cost is extra LLM calls and latency, so you gate the loop and cap iterations.
fj: Agentic RAG just means calling the retriever multiple times.
fs: It's a control loop: CRAG grades retrieved docs and takes a corrective path (rewrite, re-retrieve, web-fallback) when they're weak; Self-RAG uses reflection tokens to retrieve and self-check mid-generation. Naive RAG can't recover from one bad retrieval — these can.

followups.q: How do you stop an agentic RAG loop from running forever or exploding cost?
followups.a: Cap iterations with a hard max (e.g., 3 retrieval rounds), add a confidence/grade-based early exit, and gate entry to the loop behind a query classifier so simple lookups skip agentic behavior entirely. Each loop iteration is extra LLM and retrieval calls, so an uncapped loop on an ambiguous query can spiral. Budget the loop explicitly and log iteration counts to catch pathological queries.

followups.q: How does Self-RAG differ mechanically from CRAG?
followups.a: Self-RAG trains the model to emit reflection tokens that decide when to retrieve and whether each generated segment is supported and relevant — the control logic lives inside the model. CRAG is an external pipeline: a separate lightweight evaluator grades retrieved docs and triggers a corrective path. CRAG bolts onto any model; Self-RAG needs special training but self-corrects mid-generation.

followups.q: What does CRAG's web-search fallback risk, and how do you contain it?
followups.a: Falling back to web search pulls in untrusted, possibly contradictory or malicious content — re-introducing indirect prompt injection and freshness/accuracy risk into a curated corpus. Contain it by delimiting web results as untrusted data, sanitizing, citing them distinctly so users know the source isn't the vetted KB, and only triggering the fallback when the grade is genuinely 'incorrect.'

q: What is the lost-in-the-middle problem, and concretely how do you order retrieved chunks in the prompt to counter it?
a: LLMs attend most strongly to the start and end of their context and systematically underweight the middle — a U-shaped recall curve driven by causal masking and positional encodings like RoPE. So even when the right chunk is retrieved, burying it in the middle of a large context can make the model miss it. The practical fix is to retrieve fewer, higher-quality chunks (rerank first) so there's less middle to lose, and to reorder by relevance into a 'V': put the most relevant chunk first, the next most relevant last, and the weakest ones in the middle. This is cheap, model-agnostic, and often lifts answer accuracy more than swapping models. Verify it on your data, since the exact curve varies by model.
fj: If the right chunk is in the context window, the model will use it.
fs: LLMs underweight the middle of the context — a U-shaped recall curve. I rerank to fewer chunks, then place the most relevant first and the next-best last so the strongest evidence sits where attention is highest.

code.label: V-shaped reorder
lines: # best at ends, weakest in the middle

followups.q: What actually causes the U-shaped attention curve mechanistically?
followups.a: It stems from how decoder-only transformers handle position: causal masking and encodings like RoPE give recency a boost (end of context), while early tokens benefit from being attended to by everything after them. The middle gets neither advantage. It's reinforced by training data where salient info sits at boundaries. The exact curve varies by model and improves with long-context training.

followups.q: Does reordering still matter if you only retrieve 3 chunks?
followups.a: Much less — with a short context there's barely any 'middle' to lose, which is exactly why aggressive reranking to a small k is the first-line fix. Reordering matters most when you're forced to pass many chunks (10+) and can't trim further. For small k, getting the right chunk retrieved at all dominates; positional games are a marginal second-order effect.

followups.q: How do you verify the V-shaped ordering actually helps for your model?
followups.a: Run an A/B on your eval set: same retrieved chunks, compare relevance-ordered vs V-shaped vs random ordering, measuring answer accuracy/faithfulness. The curve differs per model — some newer long-context models have largely flattened it, in which case reordering is wasted effort. Never assume; the cheap experiment tells you whether positional placement moves your numbers.

q: In RAGAS, distinguish context precision, context recall, and answer relevancy. Which need ground truth and what does each catch?
a: Context precision asks whether the relevant chunks are ranked near the top of the retrieved set — it's a reference-free signal of retriever ranking quality and rewards putting good chunks first. Context recall asks whether the retrieved context contains everything needed to produce the reference answer; it requires a ground-truth answer because RAGAS checks how many claims of that answer are attributable to the context, and it's the metric that catches missing evidence. Answer relevancy measures whether the generated answer actually addresses the question rather than rambling or going off-topic, and is reference-free. Together with faithfulness they form the standard quad: precision and recall diagnose retrieval, relevancy and faithfulness diagnose generation, so a low score points you at the right layer.
fj: Context precision and context recall measure the same thing.
fs: Precision is about ranking the good chunks high (reference-free); recall is about whether all needed evidence was retrieved (needs ground truth). Low recall means evidence is missing; low precision means it's there but buried under noise.

followups.q: How can RAGAS compute these metrics without a human-written reference answer for most of them?
followups.a: Three of the quad are reference-free via LLM judges: context precision checks whether each retrieved chunk is relevant, answer relevancy generates questions from the answer and measures similarity to the query, and faithfulness verifies each answer claim against context. Only context recall needs a ground-truth answer, since it checks the context covers that answer's claims.

followups.q: If context precision and context recall are both low, what's your first move?
followups.a: That points squarely at retrieval, not generation. Low recall means the right evidence isn't being fetched (fix chunking, add hybrid search, query rewriting) and low precision means the set is noisy (add a reranker, trim k). Fix recall first — a reranker can't reorder evidence that was never retrieved. Re-measure both before touching the generation prompt.

followups.q: How reliable are these LLM-judged RAGAS scores and how do you trust them?
followups.a: They're noisy and judge-model-dependent, so treat them as relative signals across changes, not absolute truths. Calibrate by correlating the judge against human labels on a sample, use a different model family as judge than as generator to avoid correlated bias, and watch for drift. Great for catching CI regressions; weaker for fine-grained absolute quality claims.

q: How does semantic caching reduce RAG latency and cost, and what's the danger of setting the similarity threshold too loose?
a: Semantic caching stores past query-answer pairs keyed by the query embedding; a new query embeds, does a similarity lookup against the cache, and if a prior query is close enough it returns the cached answer, skipping retrieval and generation entirely — turning a multi-second pipeline into a low-latency vector lookup and cutting LLM spend on repeated questions. The risk is the threshold: too loose and two semantically different questions ('reset password' vs 'reset 2FA') collide, serving a confidently wrong cached answer. So you tune the threshold conservatively, scope the cache per tenant and per document version, and invalidate on knowledge-base updates, because a stale cache silently serves answers grounded in deprecated documents.
fj: I'll cache every answer keyed by query similarity to speed things up.
fs: Semantic caching helps, but a loose threshold makes near-miss queries return the wrong cached answer. I tune the threshold conservatively, scope the cache per tenant and doc version, and invalidate it on index updates so it never serves stale grounding.

followups.q: How do you choose the similarity threshold for a semantic cache safely?
followups.a: Bias strict — a high threshold (e.g., 0.95+) so only near-identical intents hit. Calibrate it on a labeled set of paraphrase pairs and known false-collision pairs ('reset password' vs 'reset 2FA'), choosing the value that keeps the false-hit rate near zero even at the cost of a lower hit rate. A wrong cached answer served confidently is far costlier than a cache miss.

followups.q: How do you keep a semantic cache from serving answers grounded in deprecated documents?
followups.a: Tie cache entries to a knowledge-base version or document hash and invalidate (or TTL-expire) them on any index update that touches the relevant documents. Without versioned invalidation, the cache silently keeps returning answers built on content you've since corrected or deleted. Scope entries per tenant too, so one tenant's cached answer never leaks into another's results.

followups.q: Why scope the cache per tenant, and what breaks if you don't?
followups.a: Because the same question can have tenant-specific answers (different documents, permissions, configs), and access control. A global cache would let tenant A's grounded answer be served to tenant B — both a correctness bug and a data-leak/security violation. Keying the cache by tenant (and document-version) keeps answers isolated and prevents cross-tenant exposure.

q: How do you keep a RAG index fresh as source documents change, without re-embedding the whole corpus every time?
a: Full re-indexing doesn't scale, so you run an incremental pipeline keyed on a content hash per chunk: on each sync, diff sources, and only embed and upsert chunks whose hash changed, delete chunks for removed documents, and leave unchanged chunks alone. Store a document version and updated-at timestamp in chunk metadata so you can answer freshness questions and invalidate the semantic cache for touched documents. Handle the embedding-model upgrade case explicitly — switching models means every vector must be recomputed, so you build the new index in parallel and cut over, never mix vector spaces. For high-velocity sources, decouple ingestion with a queue so writes don't block retrieval, and monitor index lag as an SLO.
fj: When documents change I just rebuild the whole vector index nightly.
fs: Full rebuilds waste compute and add lag. I hash each chunk and only upsert what changed, delete chunks for removed docs, and version metadata so the cache invalidates. A model swap is the one case that forces a full re-embed — done as a parallel cutover.

followups.q: Why is an embedding-model swap the one case that forces a full re-embed?
followups.a: Different models produce vectors in incompatible spaces — a query embedded by the new model is meaningless against documents embedded by the old one, so similarity is garbage if you mix them. There's no incremental shortcut; every chunk must be recomputed. You build the new index in parallel and atomically cut over, never serving queries from a half-migrated index with two vector spaces.

followups.q: How do you handle high-velocity sources so ingestion doesn't block retrieval?
followups.a: Decouple writes from reads with a queue: source changes enqueue embedding/upsert jobs processed by background workers, while the live index keeps serving queries. This makes the index eventually consistent — chunks are stale for the queue's drain time. Track that staleness as an index-lag SLO and alert when it exceeds your freshness requirement.

followups.q: What's the role of the per-chunk content hash beyond skipping unchanged work?
followups.a: The hash is the change-detection primitive: comparing the stored hash to the recomputed one tells you precisely which chunks changed, so you upsert only those and leave the rest untouched. It also gives you idempotent re-runs (re-processing a document twice is a no-op) and a clean signal for invalidating the semantic cache for exactly the touched chunks.

q: A user asks a multi-hop question ('which of our vendors in the EU had an outage last quarter?'). Why does single-shot retrieval fail and how do you fix it?
a: Single-shot retrieval embeds the whole question and grabs top-k, but a multi-hop question needs facts that live in different documents — the EU vendor list in one place, outage records in another — and no single chunk is similar to the full compound query, so recall collapses. The fix is query decomposition: an LLM breaks the question into sub-queries ('list EU vendors', 'which vendors had outages last quarter'), retrieves for each independently, and the answer is composed from the union, optionally iteratively where the answer to hop one feeds hop two. This is the retrieval side of agentic RAG. Gate decomposition behind a classifier so simple lookups stay one-shot and only genuinely compound queries pay the extra LLM and retrieval round-trips.
fj: I'd just increase k so the retriever grabs everything it needs.
fs: A compound query isn't similar to any single chunk, so raising k adds noise, not the missing hop. I decompose into sub-queries, retrieve for each, and compose — gated behind a classifier so simple queries stay one-shot.

followups.q: When do you need iterative (sequential) decomposition rather than parallel sub-queries?
